skill.name: routeros-qemu-chr
skill.description: Guides users through running MikroTik RouterOS CHR in QEMU, including image selection, boot modes, VirtIO devices, port forwarding, CI usage, and common boot issues.
skill.summary: Run and troubleshoot MikroTik RouterOS CHR virtual machines with QEMU.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, and filesystem patterns. Review found these are mostly documentation examples for launching RouterOS CHR in QEMU, but several examples require privileged host changes, external downloads, persistent processes, and local service exposure. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Host Configuration in CI Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The CI reference installs packages with sudo and changes /dev/kvm permissions to mode 0666. This is legitimate for QEMU acceleration on GitHub runners, but it weakens host device access if copied outside an isolated CI runner.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The privileged commands and world-writable device rule are explicit. The context is CI setup, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Persistent QEMU Process and PID Cleanup Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples launch QEMU with nohup in the background and store a PID under /tmp. This is expected for VM testing, but copied commands can leave long-running processes or kill the wrong process if PID handling is unsafe.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The persistent process pattern is directly documented and paired with cleanup commands. The documentation also explains the PID risk, which lowers concern about intent.

medium_findings.title: Local RouterOS Services Exposed Through Port Forwarding
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The QEMU examples forward RouterOS WebFig, REST, SSH, API, API-SSL, and WinBox ports to host ports. These examples are useful for local labs, but users must avoid exposing them on shared or public interfaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The host forwarding and unauthenticated root health check are explicit. They are local QEMU user networking examples, not evidence of remote exposure by default.

medium_findings.title: External Downloads Without Integrity Verification
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The references download RouterOS CHR images and extra packages from MikroTik hosts, but they do not show checksum or signature verification. The domains appear expected for MikroTik artifacts, yet users should verify images before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The downloads are clearly shown and from expected vendor domains. The concern is missing integrity guidance, not a confirmed malicious endpoint.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many static findings identify Markdown code fences and inline shell examples as Ruby backtick execution. The files are documentation and do not execute commands by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown documentation, tables, or fenced examples. No executable Ruby source file or runtime hook is present in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Domain-Term False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static weak-cryptography alerts appear on RouterOS CHR text and kernel or licensing discussion. I did not find evidence of cryptographic functions, password hashing, or custom crypto implementation in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text discusses CHR, licensing, and architecture differences. There is no crypto API use or security-sensitive algorithm selection at the cited lines.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: No suspicious override instructions, claims of pre-approval, or requests to skip security analysis were found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted review found normal skill metadata and technical documentation. This does not prove absence in unscanned files, but all static-analysis files were checked for this issue.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo and Device Permission Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes sudo package installation and udev rules that make /dev/kvm writable. Treat these as privileged host changes that should stay inside disposable CI or lab environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The privileged commands are explicit and directly tied to QEMU setup. The risk depends on where users run the copied workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Background Process Launch
dangerous_patterns.description: The CI reference starts QEMU with nohup and redirects logs to /tmp. Long-running VM processes require reliable cleanup and isolated runners.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The background launch pattern is explicit. The file also includes cleanup guidance, so this is an operational warning rather than a block.

dangerous_patterns.title: Forwarded Management Ports
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples forward RouterOS management services from the VM to host ports. Users should bind only to localhost and use credentials before using shared hosts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The forwarded service list is explicit. QEMU user networking usually binds locally, but the documentation does not fully discuss exposure boundaries.

content.value_statement: Running RouterOS CHR in QEMU can fail because boot firmware, VirtIO devices, and acceleration differ by host. This skill gives practical commands and checks for local labs and CI workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains x86_64, aarch64, and UEFI image choices for RouterOS CHR.
Provides QEMU launch patterns for shell scripts, TypeScript, and CI jobs.
Documents VirtIO disk and network choices that affect RouterOS boot success.
Describes KVM, HVF, and TCG acceleration selection across Linux and macOS.
Shows health checks, port forwarding, package upload, and cleanup patterns for CI.
Summarizes CHR licensing limits, trial behavior, and common QEMU issues.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not start or manage virtual machines automatically.
Commands may require QEMU, firmware files, host permissions, and RouterOS images.
RouterOS releases and GitHub runner capabilities can change after the skill content was written.
It does not bypass MikroTik licensing or provide production hardening guidance.

use_cases.title: Local RouterOS Lab Setup
use_cases.description: Choose the correct CHR image, QEMU firmware, and VirtIO devices for a repeatable local test router.

use_cases.title: RouterOS Integration Testing
use_cases.description: Build a GitHub Actions job that boots CHR, waits for REST readiness, runs tests, and cleans up QEMU.

use_cases.title: Boot Failure Diagnosis
use_cases.description: Identify architecture, firmware, and device model mismatches that cause silent CHR boot stalls.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a CHR Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a RouterOS CHR image for QEMU on my host. My host OS is [OS], CPU is [CPU], and I need [local testing or CI].

prompt_templates.title: Create a QEMU Launch Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a QEMU command for RouterOS CHR with [architecture], [acceleration], [firmware path], and host ports for WebFig and SSH.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a CHR Boot Problem
prompt_templates.prompt: Diagnose this RouterOS CHR QEMU boot failure. Host: [host details]. Command: [command]. Symptom or log: [symptom].

prompt_templates.title: Design a CI Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitHub Actions workflow that downloads RouterOS CHR, boots it with QEMU, waits for REST readiness, runs tests, and always cleans up.

output_examples.input: User asks why aarch64 CHR stalls after switching to if=virtio.
output_examples.output: The answer identifies the VirtIO MMIO trap, explains why RouterOS needs PCI transport, and recommends explicit virtio-blk-pci configuration.

output_examples.input: User asks for a CI plan to test a RouterOS REST client.
output_examples.output: The answer outlines QEMU installation, CHR download, KVM fallback, boot polling, REST readiness checks, test execution, and cleanup.

output_examples.input: User asks whether the free CHR license is enough for API tests.
output_examples.output: The answer explains that the free license limits forwarding throughput but is usually sufficient for REST, SSH, WebFig, and CI tests.

best_practices: Run copied QEMU and sudo examples in isolated lab or CI environments first.
Use explicit VirtIO PCI devices when booting aarch64 RouterOS CHR.
Verify downloaded RouterOS images and keep management ports bound to trusted interfaces.
anti_patterns: Do not use direct QEMU kernel boot for RouterOS CHR.
Do not assume /dev/kvm exists or is writable without checking host and guest architecture.
Do not expose fresh CHR management ports to untrusted networks.

faq.question: Does this skill run RouterOS CHR for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions and examples that you can adapt for QEMU, scripts, or CI workflows.

faq.question: Can I use the free CHR license for testing?
faq.answer: Yes. The free license has a 1 Mbps forwarding limit, but API and management tests usually work well.

faq.question: Why does aarch64 fail with if=virtio?
faq.answer: QEMU maps that shortcut to VirtIO MMIO on the virt machine, but RouterOS CHR expects VirtIO PCI.

faq.question: Does the skill cover GitHub Actions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes CI patterns for installing QEMU, starting CHR, waiting for boot, and cleanup.

faq.question: Is KVM always available?
faq.answer: No. KVM depends on the host, runner, permissions, and matching guest architecture.

faq.question: Does this skill include production security hardening?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on development, lab, and CI workflows for QEMU-based CHR instances.
